question: How many NPMs can be operated in a single Reactor Building at the NuScale power plant?

nuscale_ch01_0001.title: CHAPTER 1 INTRODUCTION AND GENERAL DESCRIPTION OF THE PLANT
nuscale_ch01_0001.summary: This section introduces the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), which is required for standard design certification under U.S. regulations. It provides an overview of the NuScale Power Module (NPM), a modular, scalable nuclear steam supply system designed for safe, flexible operation, highlighting key safety features such as passive shutdown and cooling, compact design, and advanced digital controls. The FSAR covers both individual module and multi-module plant configurations, focusing on safety analysis and regulatory compliance.

nuscale_ch01_0036.title: Refueling Operations
nuscale_ch01_0036.summary: Refueling operations for a NuScale Power Module (NPM) are conducted independently from other modules. The process involves moving the NPM from its operating bay to the refueling pool, disassembling it for inspection, maintenance, and refueling, and then reassembling and returning it to service after successful testing and startup procedures.

nuscale_ch01_0035.title: Fuel Handling and Reactor Maintenance Areas
nuscale_ch01_0035.summary: The fuel handling and reactor maintenance areas are located at the west end of the Reactor Building and include spaces for the spent fuel pool, refueling pool, and dry dock, with facilities designed for safe fuel handling and maintenance operations. These areas provide radiation protection for personnel, support the transport and storage of new and spent fuel assemblies, and include equipment such as cranes and pallet jacks to facilitate fuel movement and inspection.

nuscale_ch01_0002.title: Introduction
nuscale_ch01_0002.summary: The Introduction section outlines that this document is the Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) for the NuScale small modular reactor, submitted as part of the design certification process under 10 CFR 52. It provides an overview of the NuScale Power Module (NPM), its modular and scalable design, and highlights key safety features such as passive shutdown capability, compact containment, and advanced digital controls.

nuscale_ch01_0051.title: Plant Features of Special Interest
nuscale_ch01_0051.summary: This section describes key plant features of special interest in the NuScale Power Plant, including the Central Utility Building (CUB) and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs), which house essential but nonsafety-related systems and backup power equipment. It also highlights human factors considerations, such as fail-safe design, centralized multi-module control, digital interfaces, and workload management, all aimed at minimizing human error and meeting regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0003.title: Plant Location
nuscale_ch01_0003.summary: The Plant Location section of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report introduces the document as required for standard design certification, outlining the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design and its safety features. The NPM is a modular, scalable nuclear steam supply system that can operate from one to twelve modules within a single facility, featuring advanced safety systems that require no external power for shutdown and cooling, compact design, and comprehensive digital controls. The report details both individual module and multi-module plant designs, emphasizing the unique safety and operational characteristics of the NuScale system.

nuscale_ch01_0031.title: Plant Cooling Water Systems
nuscale_ch01_0031.summary: The plant cooling water systems at NuScale include several nonsafety-related systems essential for plant operation, such as the reactor component cooling water system (RCCWS), reactor pool and spent fuel pool cooling systems, and the circulating water system. These systems transfer heat from various plant components and pools to the site cooling water system or cooling towers, supporting efficient plant cooling and operation.

nuscale_ch01_0018.title: Principal Design Criteria
nuscale_ch01_0018.summary: The section describes the principal design criteria for the NuScale plant, focusing on the Site Cooling Water System (SCWS) and the overall facility process. It outlines the reactor core's heat transfer process, natural coolant circulation, steam generation, and the flow of superheated steam to the turbine and condenser, emphasizing the system's role in efficient heat removal and power generation.

nuscale_ch01_0032.title: Radioactive Waste Management System
nuscale_ch01_0032.summary: The section describes the radioactive waste management system at the NuScale Power Plant, noting that liquid, gaseous, and solid waste systems are detailed in Chapter 11, along with effluent monitoring. It also outlines the general arrangement of major structures, focusing on the Reactor Building (RXB), which is a reinforced, seismically designed concrete structure housing the nuclear power modules and essential systems, and provides radiation protection for personnel.

nuscale_ch01_0370.title: SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3: Control Room Area Ventilation SystemII.2 Environmental and Dynamic Effects
nuscale_ch01_0370.summary: The section confirms that the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report meets the regulatory criteria outlined in SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3, specifically regarding the control room area ventilation system’s protection against environmental and dynamic effects. The report states that there are no exceptions or deviations from the standard requirements for this aspect.

nuscale_ch01_0372.title: SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3: Control Room Area Ventilation SystemII.4 Control Room
nuscale_ch01_0372.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3 requirements for the Control Room Area Ventilation System, confirming compliance with criteria related to protection against natural phenomena, environmental effects, and system sharing. The control room ventilation system does not use emergency filtration for accident conditions; instead, it provides clean air via compressed air tanks to ensure operator safety. The review also notes that up to 12 modules share the same control room, and the system meets all relevant regulatory standards except for aspects related to ESF atmosphere cleanup systems.

edges.description: Section B describes operational procedures for individual NPMs within the reactor pool, which are part of the overall plant design and operational limits introduced in Section A.

Section A provides the general description and regulatory context for the NuScale plant design, while Section B offers specific details about the physical arrangement and maintenance areas for NPMs, with both sections together informing the operational configuration (including the number of NPMs) within a single Reactor Building.

Section A describes the independence of refueling operations for each NPM, while Section B details the physical infrastructure (pipe fittings, electrical connections, and maintenance areas) that supports such operations, indicating that the facility is designed to accommodate multiple NPMs being serviced or operated independently.
Section A provides specific operational details about NPM refueling independence within the reactor building, which supports the general plant description and design bases outlined in Section B.
Section A provides specific details about the fuel handling and reactor maintenance areas, which are part of the general plant description introduced in Section B.

Section B describes the independent refueling operations of individual NPMs within the reactor building, which provides operational context that supports the general plant description and design bases outlined in Section A.
Section B provides specific details about the location and arrangement of fuel handling and reactor maintenance areas, which are part of the general plant description introduced in Section A.

Section A discusses the operational flexibility of multiple NPMs per plant as permitted by the site license, while Section B introduces the FSAR as the document that describes the design bases and operational limits, thus Section B provides the regulatory framework within which Section A's operational details are defined.

Section A describes that multiple NPMs can be operated per plant and individually managed, while Section B explains that refueling operations for one NPM do not affect the operation of others, both supporting the concept of multiple independently operable NPMs in a single Reactor Building.

Section A describes the operational flexibility of multiple NPMs per plant as permitted by the site license, while Section B details the physical infrastructure (fuel handling and maintenance areas) that supports installation, refueling, and removal of NPMs, connecting both sections through the operational and physical accommodation of multiple NPMs in the Reactor Building.
Section A provides specific details about the plant's systems and facility layout, which are elaborations of the general plant description and design bases introduced in Section B.

Section B describes operational procedures for refueling individual NPMs within the reactor building, which presupposes the facility layout and systems described in Section A, indicating that the design and arrangement in Section A enable the independent operation and refueling described in Section B.

Section A provides a general description of the reactor facility and its systems, while Section B details the specific areas and provisions for handling and maintaining NPMs within the facility, connecting through their shared focus on facility layout and operational provisions for NPMs.

Section A references the general plant layout and points to figures and sections that may contain information about the arrangement of NPMs, while Section B introduces the FSAR and states that it includes design bases and operational limits, which would encompass the maximum number of NPMs per Reactor Building.

Section B describes operational independence and movement logistics for individual NPMs within the reactor building, which presupposes the general plant layout and arrangement of major structures and equipment described in Section A.

Section A provides a general description of the plant layout and references a figure showing the arrangement of major structures, while Section B specifies the location and function of the fuel handling and reactor maintenance areas within that layout, connecting the general arrangement to specific operational areas relevant to NPM operation.

Section B explains that multiple NPMs can be operated per plant and managed individually, which provides operational context that supports the general plant description in Section A regarding the facility's design and operational limits.

Section A provides the overall purpose and scope of the FSAR, while Section B gives specific design details about plant systems and facility layout, both contributing contextual information necessary to determine the number of NPMs that can be operated in a single Reactor Building.

Section A provides a general introduction to the NuScale FSAR and its purpose, while Section B begins to describe the general arrangement of major plant structures, which may include information relevant to the number of NPMs per Reactor Building.

Section A discusses the operation of multiple NPMs per plant and their incremental service based on site license, while Section B describes the site-specific design and operation of the cooling water system that supports these NPMs, connecting them through the dependency of NPM operation on adequate cooling system design.

Section A describes the operation and serviceability of multiple NPMs per plant as permitted by the site license, while Section B references the general arrangement and layout of major structures, which would include the spatial and systems constraints relevant to how many NPMs can be housed in a single Reactor Building.

Section A describes the principal design criteria and general plant description, while Section B outlines the radioactive waste management system and references the general arrangement of major structures, both providing complementary details about the plant layout but neither directly addresses or constrains the number of NPMs per Reactor Building.

Section A references regulatory criteria and conformance for specific systems (such as the Control Room Area Ventilation System), while Section B provides the general description and design bases of the NuScale plant, which includes the operational limits (such as the number of NPMs per Reactor Building) that Section A's regulatory conformance would apply to.

Section B describes the independent refueling operations of individual NPMs within the reactor building, while Section A outlines regulatory conformance for shared systems (such as ventilation) that support safe operation of multiple NPMs, thus Section A's requirements set the operational and safety context within which Section B's described activities occur.

Section A outlines regulatory conformance and requirements for systems such as the Control Room Area Ventilation System, while Section B describes the physical layout and operational features of the fuel handling and reactor maintenance areas, both of which are connected by their relevance to the number and arrangement of NPMs that can be accommodated and safely operated within a single Reactor Building.

Section A references regulatory criteria and conformance for specific systems (such as the Control Room Area Ventilation System) that are detailed in the broader plant design and operational limits described in Section B.

Section B describes the independent refueling operations of individual NPMs within the reactor building, which is relevant to Section A's discussion of shared systems and structures (such as ventilation) that must accommodate multiple NPMs, thus connecting operational independence to regulatory requirements for shared facility systems.

Section A discusses regulatory conformance for shared systems and structures, including operation of the Control Room Ventilation System (CRVS), while Section B describes the physical layout and provisions for handling multiple NPMs in the reactor building, connecting them through the need to ensure that shared systems (like CRVS) can support the number of NPMs present.

Section A addresses environmental and dynamic effects relevant to the control room area ventilation system, while Section B addresses the control room itself, with both sections referencing the same regulatory criteria (SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3) and thus are structurally connected by their shared regulatory context.
